clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman presents for a routine well-woman check. She has been sexually active since age 18, has had 3 sexual partners in total, and smokes 10 cigarettes per day. Her last cervical smear was 5 years ago and was reported as normal. She has no vaginal discharge, intermenstrual bleeding, or pelvic pain. Examination is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in her primary care management?
options: Reassure and repeat cervical smear in 5 years
Perform a cervical smear today and repeat in 3 years if normal
Refer directly for colposcopy given her smoking history
Order HPV DNA testing only, with no smear needed
Advise HPV vaccination alone as she is under 35
explanation: Routine cervical cancer screening for women aged 25–64 recommends a Pap smear (or co-test) at regular intervals; a 5-year gap in a 34-year-old is overdue for rescreening. A smear should be performed today, and if normal, she should be rescreened in 3 years as per standard intervals. Smoking is a cofactor for HPV persistence but does not by itself warrant colposcopy without a cytological or HPV indication.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Waiting another 5 years is inappropriate; her last smear was 5 years ago and she is due for rescreening now.
Correct — perform the smear now and repeat in 3 years if normal per routine screening guidelines.
Colposcopy referral is not indicated without abnormal cytology or a positive high-risk HPV result; smoking alone is a risk factor but not a referral criterion.
HPV DNA testing alone without cytology is not standard practice in most primary care settings for routine screening; co-testing or smear-first protocols are used.
HPV vaccination has limited protective value once a woman is already sexually active for years and does not replace cervical screening.
clinicalPearl: Cervical screening is recommended every 3 years (or 5 years with co-testing) starting at age 25; a 5-year gap in a woman who has not been co-tested means she is overdue and needs a smear now.
keyClue: Last smear was 5 years ago in a 34-year-old — she is overdue for routine rescreening.
commonExamTrap: Candidates often focus on the smoking history and choose colposcopy, but smoking alone without cytological abnormality does not warrant referral.
missedConcept: Understanding of cervical screening intervals and what triggers colposcopy referral versus routine recall.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old postmenopausal woman presents to the primary care clinic after noticing a painless lump in her left breast for 6 weeks. She has no family history of breast or ovarian cancer, is on no medications, and her BMI is 28. On examination there is a 2 cm firm, non-tender, ill-defined mass in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast with no overlying skin changes and no axillary lymphadenopathy. Which combination of investigations would best establish the diagnosis at this stage?
options: Mammogram alone
Bilateral mammogram and breast ultrasound
Breast ultrasound alone
Fine-needle aspiration cytology alone
MRI breast without mammogram
explanation: Triple assessment for a symptomatic breast lump includes clinical examination, imaging (mammogram ± ultrasound), and tissue sampling. In a postmenopausal woman over 40, bilateral mammography is the primary imaging modality; ultrasound is added to characterise the lesion further (solid vs. cystic) and evaluate the axilla. Mammogram alone may miss some lesions without ultrasound characterisation, especially in dense breast tissue.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Mammography alone is insufficient; ultrasound adds important characterisation of the mass and axillary nodes.
Correct — bilateral mammogram combined with targeted breast ultrasound forms the imaging component of triple assessment for a symptomatic postmenopausal woman.
Ultrasound alone is preferred in women under 35; in a 52-year-old the mammogram is essential and should not be omitted.
Fine-needle aspiration cytology is the tissue-sampling component of triple assessment, not a substitute for imaging which must come first.
MRI is reserved for specific indications (high-risk screening, implants, extent of disease pre-surgery) and is not the initial investigation for a symptomatic lump.
clinicalPearl: Triple assessment (clinical + imaging + cytology/histology) is the gold standard for any symptomatic breast lump; in women over 40, bilateral mammography with ultrasound forms the imaging step.
keyClue: Postmenopausal woman with a firm, ill-defined, painless breast lump — requires full imaging triple assessment.
commonExamTrap: Choosing ultrasound alone, which is appropriate only in women under 35 with a palpable lump.
missedConcept: Triple assessment protocol and the age-based preference for mammography over ultrasound as the primary modality.

clinicalStem: A primary care nurse is about to perform a wound dressing change on a patient with a methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA)-positive leg ulcer. The patient is alert and sitting in the treatment room. Which combination of personal protective equipment (PPE) and infection control measures is most appropriate for this procedure?
options: Gloves only; no other PPE required for a wound dressing
Gloves and apron; perform hand hygiene before and after the procedure
Gloves, apron, and surgical mask; no hand hygiene needed if gloves are worn
Full aerosol precautions including N95 respirator, gloves, gown, and eye protection
Apron only; gloves are unnecessary as the wound is not actively bleeding
explanation: Standard and contact precautions for a known MRSA wound require gloves and a plastic apron to protect clothing from contamination, plus hand hygiene (5 moments) before and after the procedure. A surgical mask and eye protection are not routinely required for wound dressing unless there is risk of splashing. Wearing gloves does not replace hand hygiene.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Gloves alone are insufficient; an apron is also needed to protect uniform from MRSA contamination during wound care.
Correct — gloves, apron, and rigorous hand hygiene before and after represent appropriate standard plus contact precautions for MRSA wound care.
Gloves never substitute for hand hygiene; wearing gloves then failing to perform hand hygiene after removal is a key source of cross-infection.
Full aerosol precautions (N95, gown, eye protection) are reserved for airborne or droplet transmission risks (e.g., TB, COVID-19); MRSA wound care does not generate aerosols.
Gloves are mandatory when in contact with any wound or body fluid regardless of bleeding status.
clinicalPearl: Gloves never replace hand hygiene — MRSA contact precautions require gloves, apron, and the WHO 5-moment hand hygiene protocol.
keyClue: MRSA-positive wound dressing — contact precautions mandating gloves, apron, and hand hygiene.
commonExamTrap: Thinking that wearing gloves eliminates the need for hand hygiene before and after.
missedConcept: Contact precautions for MRSA and the fact that gloves are an adjunct to, not a replacement for, hand hygiene.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old medical student sustains a needlestick injury while recapping a needle after drawing blood from a patient known to be hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) positive and HIV negative. The student has not completed their hepatitis B vaccination course. She immediately washes the wound under running water. What is the most important next step in her management?
options: Commence a full 3-dose hepatitis B vaccine course and review in 1 month
Administer hepatitis B immunoglobulin (HBIG) and begin the hepatitis B vaccine course immediately
Reassure the student; hepatitis B is not transmitted by needlestick injury
Start empirical antiretroviral post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) within 72 hours
Order hepatitis B surface antibody level and act only if she is non-immune
explanation: Following a high-risk needlestick from an HBsAg-positive source in an unvaccinated or incompletely vaccinated healthcare worker, both HBIG (passive immunity) and the hepatitis B vaccine (active immunity) should be administered as soon as possible — ideally within 24–48 hours. This combined approach provides the best protection. HIV PEP is not indicated as the source patient is HIV negative.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Vaccine alone is insufficient for a high-risk exposure in an unvaccinated individual; HBIG provides immediate passive protection while the vaccine course builds active immunity.
Correct — combined HBIG plus immediate hepatitis B vaccination is the evidence-based post-exposure protocol for a significant exposure from an HBsAg-positive source in an unimmunised worker.
Hepatitis B is efficiently transmitted by percutaneous needlestick injury; this is a high-risk exposure requiring urgent action.
The source patient is documented HIV negative; empirical HIV PEP is not indicated and would expose the student to unnecessary side effects.
Waiting for serology results delays critical passive immunisation; HBIG must be given as soon as possible after high-risk exposure regardless of prior serology.
clinicalPearl: After needlestick from HBsAg-positive source in an unvaccinated worker, give HBIG plus hepatitis B vaccine immediately — do not wait for serology.
keyClue: HBsAg-positive source, incomplete vaccination, needlestick — dual prophylaxis with HBIG and vaccine required urgently.
commonExamTrap: Starting HIV PEP when the source patient is documented HIV negative.
missedConcept: The need for passive (HBIG) plus active (vaccine) immunisation after high-risk hepatitis B exposure in incompletely vaccinated individuals.

subtopic: Clinical features distinguishing monkeypox from varicella
clinicalStem: A 31-year-old man presents to a primary care clinic with a 4-day history of fever (38.8°C), severe headache, and myalgia, followed 2 days later by a rash. He recently returned from West Africa 10 days ago. Examination reveals tender bilateral inguinal and axillary lymphadenopathy. The rash consists of approximately 30 lesions distributed on the face, palms, and soles; all lesions appear to be in the same stage of development — firm, deep-seated, and umbilicated vesicles. Which diagnosis best fits this clinical picture?
options: Varicella (chickenpox)
Monkeypox
Secondary syphilis
Disseminated herpes simplex
Measles
explanation: Monkeypox is distinguished from chickenpox by: (1) prominent prodromal fever and systemic illness before rash onset, (2) marked lymphadenopathy (absent in chickenpox), (3) lesions in the same stage of development (chickenpox has lesions in multiple stages simultaneously), (4) involvement of palms and soles, (5) deep-seated umbilicated lesions, and (6) epidemiological link to West/Central Africa. All these features point to monkeypox.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Varicella lesions appear in crops at different stages (macule, papule, vesicle, crust simultaneously), do not involve palms and soles prominently, and do not cause significant lymphadenopathy — the opposite of this presentation.
Correct — the combination of West African travel, prodromal fever, prominent lymphadenopathy, palm/sole involvement, and uniform-stage deep umbilicated lesions is classic for monkeypox.
Secondary syphilis produces a non-vesicular, non-tender maculopapular rash on palms and soles but without fever prodrome, lymphadenopathy of this severity, or vesicular lesions.
Disseminated HSV typically occurs in immunocompromised patients and does not produce this geographic pattern with lymphadenopathy and palm/sole involvement.
Measles presents with Koplik's spots, coryza, conjunctivitis, and a morbilliform rash spreading from head downward — not vesicular lesions on palms and soles.
clinicalPearl: Monkeypox = fever + lymphadenopathy + same-stage deep umbilicated lesions on palms and soles after travel to endemic region; lymphadenopathy is the key differentiator from chickenpox.
keyClue: Tender lymphadenopathy + all lesions in same stage + palm/sole involvement + West Africa travel history.
commonExamTrap: Confusing with chickenpox because both produce vesicular rash — the absence of lymphadenopathy in chickenpox and the multi-stage lesion pattern are the key distinguishing features.
missedConcept: Monkeypox produces lymphadenopathy (absent in varicella) and lesions all in the same stage (varicella has multiple stages simultaneously).

subtopic: Clinical diagnosis and investigation of chickenpox
clinicalStem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to a primary care clinic by his mother. He has had low-grade fever and malaise for 1 day, followed by an intensely pruritic rash that started on his trunk and is spreading to his face and limbs. Examination shows lesions in different stages of development — macules, papules, clear vesicles, and pustules with some crusted lesions — predominantly on the trunk with relative sparing of palms and soles. There is no lymphadenopathy. He has not been vaccinated against varicella. Which single test would confirm the diagnosis if needed?
options: Varicella-zoster IgM serology
Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) for varicella-zoster virus from lesion swab
Tzanck smear of vesicle base
Full blood count showing lymphocytosis
Chest X-ray to assess for pneumonitis
explanation: PCR testing of vesicle fluid or a swab from a lesion base is the gold-standard confirmatory test for varicella-zoster virus infection, offering high sensitivity and specificity. While the clinical picture is classic for chickenpox, PCR is the most accurate single confirmatory investigation. Tzanck smear can suggest herpetic infection but cannot differentiate between HSV and VZV.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Varicella-zoster IgM may be negative early in the illness and may cross-react; serology is less reliable for acute diagnosis than direct virological testing.
Correct — PCR from a vesicle swab is the most sensitive and specific single confirmatory test for VZV.
Tzanck smear shows multinucleated giant cells indicating herpetic infection but cannot distinguish between herpes simplex and varicella-zoster virus.
Lymphocytosis on FBC is a non-specific finding in many viral infections and does not confirm VZV.
Chest X-ray is appropriate if varicella pneumonitis is suspected (usually in adults or immunocompromised patients) but is not used to confirm the diagnosis of chickenpox itself.
clinicalPearl: PCR from vesicle fluid is the gold-standard confirmatory test for varicella-zoster virus; Tzanck smear is non-specific and cannot differentiate HSV from VZV.
keyClue: Multi-stage lesions (macules, papules, vesicles, crusts simultaneously) in an unvaccinated child with truncal predominance and sparing of palms/soles.
commonExamTrap: Choosing Tzanck smear as confirmation — it identifies herpetic infection but cannot specify VZV vs. HSV.
missedConcept: PCR is preferred over serology and Tzanck smear for specific virological confirmation of chickenpox.

clinicalStem: A 25-year-old woman is bitten on the right hand by an apparently healthy stray dog in a rural area of a rabies-endemic country. The bite is a single puncture wound that breaks the skin. She has never received rabies vaccination before. She presents to the primary care centre 3 hours after the bite. Which is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Wash the wound, administer rabies vaccine series only; no immunoglobulin required for a single bite
Wash the wound thoroughly with soap and water, administer rabies immunoglobulin (RIG) into and around the wound, and begin the rabies post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) vaccine series
Observe the biting dog for 10 days; start PEP only if the dog develops signs of rabies
Administer rabies immunoglobulin systemically only; vaccine is not required for minor bites
Reassure the patient; a single puncture wound from a dog rarely transmits rabies
explanation: For an unvaccinated person with a WHO Category III exposure (bite that breaks the skin) in a rabies-endemic region, immediate management includes: (1) thorough wound washing with soap and water for 15 minutes, (2) RIG infiltrated into and around the wound (passive immunity), and (3) commencement of the rabies PEP vaccine series (active immunity). Wound washing alone reduces viral load significantly. RIG plus vaccine is mandatory for Category III exposures in unvaccinated individuals.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Vaccine alone without RIG is insufficient for an unvaccinated person with a Category III exposure; RIG provides critical immediate passive immunity during the window before the vaccine takes effect.
Correct — wound washing plus local RIG infiltration plus PEP vaccine series is the complete evidence-based response for a Category III exposure in an unvaccinated patient in an endemic area.

The 10-day observation rule applies to the dog, but PEP should be started immediately and stopped only if the dog remains healthy after 10 days — delaying PEP is dangerous as rabies is almost universally fatal once symptomatic.
RIG alone without the vaccine series does not confer lasting protection; both passive (RIG) and active (vaccine) immunisation are required.
Any bite that breaks the skin in a rabies-endemic area from an animal of unknown vaccination status must be treated as a potential rabies exposure — reassurance is dangerous and incorrect.
clinicalPearl: For Category III rabies exposure in an unvaccinated person: immediate wound washing + local RIG infiltration + full PEP vaccine series — never delay PEP waiting to observe the animal.
keyClue: Unvaccinated patient, bite breaking the skin, rabies-endemic country — Category III exposure requiring RIG plus vaccine.
commonExamTrap: Waiting 10 days to observe the dog before starting PEP — PEP should begin immediately and be discontinued only if the dog is proven healthy after 10 days.
missedConcept: RIG is essential for unvaccinated patients with Category III exposure; vaccine alone is insufficient to bridge the immunity gap in the early days after exposure.
redFlagReason: Rabies is virtually 100% fatal once clinical disease develops; delay in PEP initiation risks a uniformly lethal outcome.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old farmer presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever, agitation, and bizarre behaviour. Two months ago he was bitten on the leg by a wild bat on his farm; the wound was minor and he did not seek medical attention. Examination reveals: temperature 38.6°C, extreme agitation, hydrophobia (painful spasms when offered water), aerophobia (spasms triggered by a gentle fan), profuse salivation, and alternating periods of lucidity and confusion. Which investigation is most useful for confirming the diagnosis?
options: CT brain with contrast
Lumbar puncture for CSF analysis
Direct fluorescent antibody (DFA) test on skin biopsy from the nape of the neck
Serum rabies neutralising antibody titres
MRI brain showing brainstem abnormalities
explanation: In a clinically suspected case of rabies encephalitis, the most specific antemortem confirmatory test is the direct fluorescent antibody (DFA) test on a full-thickness skin biopsy from the nape of the neck (containing nerve fibres around hair follicles where the virus can be detected). RT-PCR on saliva or CSF is also used. CSF analysis and CT/MRI are non-specific. Serum antibody titres are unreliable antemortem in unvaccinated patients.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CT brain may show non-specific changes or be normal; it does not confirm rabies and is used mainly to exclude other diagnoses.
CSF analysis in rabies typically shows mild pleocytosis and elevated protein — non-specific findings that do not confirm the diagnosis.
Correct — DFA testing on a skin biopsy from the nape of the neck detects rabies virus antigen in cutaneous nerve endings and is the most specific antemortem diagnostic test.
Serum rabies neutralising antibody titres are useful in vaccinated individuals but are unreliable for diagnosing acute disease in unvaccinated patients and may not rise until late.
MRI may show T2 signal in brainstem and basal ganglia but these findings are non-specific and do not confirm rabies virus.
clinicalPearl: Antemortem diagnosis of rabies: DFA or RT-PCR on skin biopsy from the nape of the neck — detecting virus in cutaneous nerve endings around hair follicles.
keyClue: Bat bite + 2-month incubation + hydrophobia + aerophobia + hypersalivation = classic furious rabies requiring DFA skin biopsy for confirmation.
commonExamTrap: Choosing MRI or CSF as the confirmatory test — both are non-specific; the skin biopsy DFA is specific for rabies antigen.
missedConcept: The nape-of-neck skin biopsy for DFA testing is the standard antemortem confirmatory investigation for suspected rabies encephalitis.
redFlagReason: Clinical rabies encephalitis is virtually universally fatal; recognition is critical for infection control, family counselling, and identifying other potential exposures.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman at 18 weeks gestation presents with a 2-day history of pruritic vesicular rash consistent with primary varicella infection. She is uncertain whether she had chickenpox as a child. She has no dyspnoea, chest pain, or neurological symptoms. She is worried about the effects on her baby. Which investigation is most important to assess fetal risk at this stage of gestation?
options: Varicella-zoster IgG serology to confirm maternal primary infection
Urgent fetal anomaly ultrasound scan at 16–20 weeks to assess for fetal varicella syndrome
Amniocentesis for VZV PCR to diagnose fetal infection
Maternal chest X-ray to exclude varicella pneumonia
Fetal MRI to assess for cortical brain abnormalities
explanation: Maternal varicella infection between 13–20 weeks gestation carries risk of congenital varicella syndrome (CVS) — characterised by limb hypoplasia, skin scarring, eye defects, and brain abnormalities. At 18 weeks, the most important investigation is a detailed fetal anomaly ultrasound at around 16–20 weeks (or 5 weeks after infection) to detect structural abnormalities suggesting CVS. This guides counselling and further management. Amniocentesis is considered only if ultrasound shows abnormalities.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Maternal VZV IgG serology confirms the diagnosis of primary infection but does not assess fetal risk — the clinical picture already makes primary varicella likely.
Correct — a detailed fetal anomaly ultrasound scan is the key investigation to detect structural features of congenital varicella syndrome and assess fetal risk.
Amniocentesis for VZV PCR is not the first-line investigation; it is considered only when ultrasound abnormalities raise concern, as the procedure carries procedural risk.
Chest X-ray is indicated only if she develops respiratory symptoms suggesting varicella pneumonia — she currently has none.
Fetal MRI is a second-line imaging tool used when ultrasound findings are inconclusive; it is not the primary investigation at this stage.
clinicalPearl: Primary varicella at 13–20 weeks gestation carries risk of congenital varicella syndrome — a detailed anomaly ultrasound 5 weeks after infection is the key next investigation.
keyClue: 18 weeks gestation + primary varicella infection — peak risk window for congenital varicella syndrome requiring fetal anomaly ultrasound.
commonExamTrap: Jumping to amniocentesis as the definitive test — ultrasound comes first and amniocentesis is reserved for ultrasound-detected abnormalities.
missedConcept: Congenital varicella syndrome risk peaks with infection between 13–20 weeks; anomaly ultrasound is the appropriate initial fetal assessment.

clinicalStem: A primary care physician is asked to assess a 45-year-old man who has returned from South-East Asia with a 3-week history of productive cough, night sweats, and 6 kg weight loss. Chest X-ray shows a right upper lobe cavitary lesion. Sputum smear microscopy reveals acid-fast bacilli. While awaiting transfer to the respiratory unit, which isolation precaution is most appropriate in the primary care setting?
options: Standard precautions only; pulmonary tuberculosis is not contagious until confirmed by culture
Droplet precautions with surgical mask for all staff entering the room
Airborne precautions: negative-pressure room, N95 respirator for staff, surgical mask on patient for transport
Contact precautions only; the organism is transmitted by direct contact with respiratory secretions
Cohort the patient with other coughing patients in the waiting area to avoid over-using PPE
explanation: Pulmonary tuberculosis is transmitted by airborne particles (droplet nuclei <5 µm) that can remain suspended for hours. A smear-positive case is highly contagious and requires airborne precautions: a negative-pressure isolation room, N95 (or equivalent) respirators for staff entering the room, and a surgical mask on the patient when outside the room (e.g., during transport). Surgical masks on staff are insufficient for airborne transmission.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A positive sputum smear in the appropriate clinical context is sufficient to implement airborne precautions immediately — culture confirmation is not required before isolating the patient.
Surgical masks filter droplets >5 µm but do not protect against the small airborne droplet nuclei of M. tuberculosis; N95 respirators are required.
Correct — airborne precautions with negative-pressure room and N95 respirators are required for smear-positive pulmonary TB.
TB is airborne, not primarily a contact-transmitted pathogen; contact precautions alone are insufficient.
Cohorting a TB suspect with other patients would risk infecting them — isolation in a separate negative-pressure room is mandatory.
clinicalPearl: Pulmonary TB requires airborne precautions (negative-pressure room + N95 respirator) not merely droplet precautions — surgical masks for staff are inadequate.
keyClue: Acid-fast bacilli on sputum smear + cavitary upper lobe lesion = smear-positive pulmonary TB requiring immediate airborne isolation.
commonExamTrap: Choosing droplet precautions with surgical mask — TB transmits via airborne droplet nuclei requiring N95 respirators and negative-pressure rooms.
missedConcept: Airborne transmission (TB, measles, VZV) requires N95 respirators and negative-pressure rooms; droplet precautions with surgical masks are insufficient.

clinicalStem: A 49-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of irregular periods, hot flushes, night sweats, and mood changes. Her last period was 8 weeks ago. She is non-smoker, has no significant past medical history, and is on no regular medications. She wants to know whether she is entering menopause. Which single investigation would best confirm the diagnosis of perimenopause in a primary care setting?
options: Serum oestradiol level
Pelvic ultrasound to assess endometrial thickness
Serum follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) level on day 2–5 of the cycle
Thyroid function tests (TSH) to exclude thyroid dysfunction
24-hour urinary catecholamines to exclude phaeochromocytoma
explanation: In women approaching menopause, rising FSH (due to loss of negative feedback from the failing ovary) is the most useful hormonal marker. A raised FSH (typically >30–40 IU/L) on day 2–5 of the cycle in the context of oligomenorrhoea and climacteric symptoms confirms perimenopause. However, in clinical practice, the diagnosis is primarily clinical in women over 45 with typical symptoms; FSH is the most targeted confirmatory investigation when doubt remains. Thyroid function tests are important to exclude hypothyroidism as a mimic, but FSH is specific to the diagnosis of perimenopause.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Serum oestradiol fluctuates widely in perimenopause and is not a reliable diagnostic marker; a single oestradiol level can be misleadingly normal even in early perimenopause.
Pelvic ultrasound assesses for structural causes of irregular bleeding but does not diagnose perimenopause or measure ovarian reserve.
Correct — serum FSH on day 2–5 of the cycle is the most targeted investigation; a raised level in the context of oligomenorrhoea and climacteric symptoms confirms perimenopause.
Thyroid function tests (TSH) are important to exclude hypothyroidism, which can mimic menopausal symptoms, but are not specific for diagnosing perimenopause.
24-hour urinary catecholamines are indicated only if phaeochromocytoma is suspected (episodic hypertension, sweating, headache triad); this is not the appropriate first test here.
clinicalPearl: Serum FSH on day 2–5 of the cycle is the most useful hormonal investigation for perimenopause; oestradiol fluctuates too widely to be reliable as a single measurement.
keyClue: 49-year-old with irregular periods, hot flushes, and night sweats — perimenopause suspected; FSH is the targeted confirmatory test.
commonExamTrap: Ordering oestradiol rather than FSH — oestradiol levels are too variable in perimenopause to be diagnostically useful.
missedConcept: FSH rises as the ovary fails due to loss of negative feedback; this is the most reliable single hormonal marker for perimenopause.

subtopic: Indications and schedule for pre-exposure vaccination
clinicalStem: A 26-year-old veterinary student is preparing for a 6-month placement at a wildlife sanctuary in a rabies-endemic country. She has no prior rabies vaccination. She presents to a travel medicine clinic 6 weeks before departure. Which pre-exposure prophylaxis schedule is most appropriate for her?
options: Single dose of rabies vaccine; a booster will be given on arrival in the endemic country
Three doses of rabies vaccine on days 0, 7, and 21 (or 28) before departure
Rabies immunoglobulin alone; the vaccine is only needed after an actual exposure
Two doses of rabies vaccine on days 0 and 7; a third dose only if bitten
No vaccine required; standard wound care and PEP after any animal contact is sufficient
explanation: Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) for rabies consists of three intramuscular doses of cell-culture rabies vaccine given on days 0, 7, and 21 or 28. This is recommended for individuals at high ongoing risk — including veterinary workers, animal handlers, and travellers spending extended periods in endemic areas. PrEP simplifies post-exposure management (fewer vaccine doses needed, no RIG required) and provides a safety net when post-exposure care may not be immediately available.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A single dose does not provide adequate pre-exposure immunity; three doses are required to complete the primary series.
Correct — three doses on days 0, 7, and 21 or 28 complete the pre-exposure prophylaxis series for this high-risk traveller.
RIG is a component of post-exposure prophylaxis (passive immunity) and has no role in pre-exposure prophylaxis.
Only two pre-exposure doses leaves the series incomplete; the full three-dose schedule is required for adequate seroconversion.
For a veterinary worker in an endemic country, relying solely on post-exposure care is unacceptable — access to timely PEP and RIG may be unavailable, and the delay can be fatal.
clinicalPearl: Rabies pre-exposure prophylaxis is 3 doses on days 0, 7, and 21/28; it simplifies post-exposure management and eliminates the need for RIG if subsequently bitten.
keyClue: Veterinary worker with extended time in endemic country — high-risk occupation requiring full 3-dose pre-exposure prophylaxis series.
commonExamTrap: Assuming 2 doses are sufficient for pre-exposure prophylaxis — 3 doses are required for the complete primary series.
missedConcept: Pre-exposure rabies vaccination requires 3 doses; its major advantage is that post-exposure management is simplified (only 2 booster vaccines, no RIG needed).
